title: Turn Rough Notes into a Clear One-Page Class Handout
content:
Take my rough notes and turn them into a one-page handout for students.

Requirements:
- Audience: [grade/level + subject]
- Goal: Students should understand and be able to do: [learning objective]
- Keep it concise, plain language, and classroom-ready.
- Do not add new concepts I didn’t mention; you may add tiny bridging phrases only.

Output format:
1) Title
2) “What you need to know” (3–6 bullets)
3) Key terms (up to 6, each with a one-line definition)
4) Worked example (if my notes include procedures/problems; otherwise skip)
5) Common mistakes (3 bullets)
6) Quick check (5 short questions: mix of multiple-choice and short answer)
7) Answer key

Here are my notes (messy is fine):
[paste notes here]

title: Write a concise product landing page hero section (headline, subhead, CTA)
content:
Write a landing page hero section for the product described below.

Product details:
- Product name: [Name]
- What it is (1 sentence): [Description]
- Target customer: [Audience]
- Main pain point: [Pain]
- Key benefit/outcome: [Benefit]
- Top 3 features (bullets): [Feature 1], [Feature 2], [Feature 3]
- Proof/trust signals: [Metrics, customers, testimonials, compliance]
- Tone: professional, concise

Output requirements:
- Headline: 6–10 words, clear outcome-focused (no hype)
- Subheadline: 1–2 sentences, specify who it’s for + how it helps
- 3 benefit bullets (not features), each 6–10 words
- Primary CTA button text (2–4 words) + secondary CTA (2–4 words)
- One sentence of microcopy under CTA that reduces friction (e.g., “No credit card required.”)
- Provide 3 alternative headlines at the end.

Constraints:
- Avoid jargon, exclamation points, and vague claims (“best”, “revolutionary”).
- Use plain language and concrete outcomes.

title: Friendly Instagram Carousel: 5 Study Tips With Clean Icons on Pastel Gradient
content:
Design a 5-slide Instagram carousel that teaches simple study tips in a friendly, helpful style.

Visual style: clean modern vector illustration, soft pastel gradient backgrounds (mint → lavender), rounded shapes, subtle grain texture, lots of white space, high contrast for readability, consistent icon set (line icons with rounded corners), gentle drop shadows.

Format: 1080x1350 (4:5) for each slide, safe margins, space at the bottom for UI. Keep text minimal and bold.

Slide-by-slide composition:
1) Cover: big title “5 Study Tips That Actually Work” + small subtitle “Save this for later” + friendly character holding a notebook, confetti-like dots.
2) Tip 1: “Study in 25-minute sprints” (Pomodoro) + timer icon + small progress bar.
3) Tip 2: “Test yourself, don’t reread” + flashcard icon + checkmarks.
4) Tip 3: “Explain it like you’re teaching” + speech bubble + simple diagram doodle.
5) Tip 4 & 5 split: left “Plan tomorrow in 2 minutes” (calendar icon) right “Sleep = memory” (moon icon) + tiny footer “@classmind”.

Typography: bold rounded sans-serif for headings, smaller regular sans-serif for subtext; ensure crisp, legible text.

Lighting/mood: bright, upbeat, approachable; no harsh shadows.

Negative prompts: photorealistic people, cluttered layout, excessive text, neon colors, messy handwriting, watermark, low-res, blurry text.

Output: five separate images, consistent layout system and color palette across all slides.

title: Write a confident, structured email that drives a clear decision
content:
Draft a confident, structured email for the situation below.

Context
- Who I’m writing to (role + relationship): [e.g., vendor account manager / internal stakeholder / client]
- What I need from them (single decision or action): [decision/action]
- Why now (deadline or trigger): [date/event]
- Background in 2–4 bullets (only what they need):
  - [bullet]
  - [bullet]
  - [bullet]
- Constraints (budget, scope, policy, dependencies): [constraints]
- My preferred outcome: [what “yes” looks like]
- Acceptable alternatives: [option B/C]
- Attachments/links to reference (if any): [items]

Requirements
- Tone: confident, structured, calm; no fluff; no apologies unless truly needed
- Keep it concise (120–220 words) but complete
- Use short paragraphs and, if helpful, a tiny bullet list
- Include a clear call to action with a specific deadline
- Avoid jargon; make it easy to skim on mobile

Output format
1) Subject line options (3), each under 60 characters
2) Email body
3) One-line follow-up message to send if no reply after 2 business days

Write the email now using my details.

title: Turn a rough business idea into a 1-page plan + next steps
content:
Take my rough business idea and turn it into a crisp 1-page plan I can share with a colleague.

My idea (messy is fine):
[Paste idea here]

Output (keep it modern and direct):
- One-sentence summary
- Target customer: who, where they are, what they struggle with
- Problem: 3 bullets (plain language)
- Solution: what we do + what we don’t do
- Value proposition: 3 punchy bullets
- How we make money: pricing options + what to test first
- Go-to-market: 5 concrete channels/tactics with first action for each
- Differentiation: 3 likely competitors/alternatives and how we’re different
- Risks & assumptions: top 5 with how to validate quickly
- 14-day plan: day-by-day actions (small team, limited budget)

Constraints:
- No buzzwords, no fluff, no long paragraphs.
- If something is unclear, make a reasonable assumption and label it “Assumption: …”.
- End with 5 questions I should answer next to tighten the plan.

title: Quick UX critique with prioritized fixes (for a screen or flow)
content:
Act as a UX designer doing a fast, practical critique.

Context:
- Product/app: [what it is]
- Target user: [who]
- User goal: [what they’re trying to do]
- Screen/flow to review: [paste copy, describe layout, or list steps]
- Constraints: [deadlines, tech limits, brand rules]

Deliver:
1) Top 5 issues you see (highest impact first). For each: what’s happening, why it’s a problem, and who it affects.
2) Recommended fixes for each issue, written as concrete UI changes (copy, layout, controls, states).
3) A “do this first” plan: 3 quick wins (≤1 day each) + 2 deeper improvements (multi-day).
4) Any missing info you’d ask for, but still make reasonable assumptions and proceed.

Keep it clear and actionable. Avoid jargon unless you define it briefly.

title: Photorealistic product hero shot for an AI classroom app (mobile UI in hand)
content: Create a photorealistic product hero image for an AI classroom app called “classmind”. Scene: a teacher’s hand holding a modern smartphone showing a clean, minimal app dashboard (no readable text, just simple UI shapes and charts). Background: softly blurred classroom with warm daylight through windows, students out of focus, subtle chalkboard texture. Composition: vertical 4:5, phone centered slightly right, shallow depth of field (bokeh), crisp focus on the phone and fingertips. Lighting: natural morning light with gentle highlights, soft shadows, neutral color grading. Style: high-end tech marketing photo, realistic skin texture, accurate reflections on glass screen, no exaggerated HDR. Add a small desk edge at bottom-left with a notebook and pen slightly blurred. Avoid logos, watermarks, extra hands, distorted fingers, or messy UI. Output: ultra-detailed, photorealistic, clean and modern.

title: Friendly coding mentor scene: cozy desk, readable code on screen, warm lighting
content:
Create a warm, friendly illustration of a coding mentor vibe in a cozy home office.

Scene & subject:
- A modern laptop on a wooden desk, screen showing a clean code editor with readable (but fictional) JavaScript/TypeScript code and helpful inline comments.
- A sticky note near the laptop that says: “You’ve got this.”
- A mug of tea/coffee, a small plant, and a simple mechanical keyboard or notebook nearby.

Composition:
- 3/4 angle from slightly above desk height, framing the laptop as the focal point.
- Shallow depth of field: screen and hands/keyboard sharp, background softly blurred.
- Subtle visual cues of learning: a small checklist page with a few items ticked.

Lighting & mood:
- Golden hour window light from the side + soft desk lamp fill.
- Friendly and helpful mood; inviting, calm, not overly dramatic.

Style:
- Semi-realistic digital art (not photoreal), clean shapes, gentle textures.
- Warm color palette (amber, soft browns, muted greens) with a touch of cool blue from the screen.

Details to avoid:
- No logos, no identifiable brand UI, no copyrighted icons.
- No messy clutter; keep it tidy and approachable.

Output:
- High resolution, crisp focus on the code and desk objects, minimal noise.

title: Weekly Productivity Reset: pick priorities, block time, and define next actions
content:
Help me do a 30-minute weekly productivity reset. Keep it confident, structured, and concise.

My context (paste and work from this):
- Roles/areas: [e.g., Work, Personal, Health]
- Big goals (next 3 months):
  1) …
  2) …
  3) …
- Fixed commitments this week (meetings, deadlines, travel):
- Current task dump (messy is fine):
- Energy constraints (sleep, workouts, caregiving, etc.):

What I want you to produce:
1) Top 3 priorities for the week (with a one-sentence “why now” for each)
2) A short “Not doing / Defer” list to protect focus
3) For each priority: 3–7 concrete next actions written as verbs (each action <= 15 minutes when possible)
4) A time-blocking plan for Mon–Sun:
   - show morning/afternoon/evening blocks
   - include buffer time and admin time
   - schedule the next actions into blocks
5) A daily checklist template (5 lines max) I can reuse
6) A risk list: what could derail me + a specific countermeasure for each

Rules:
- If something is ambiguous, make a sensible assumption and note it briefly.
- Prefer fewer, finishable commitments over exhaustive lists.
- Use bullet points and short headings only—no long paragraphs.

title: Rigorous Research Plan + Annotated Bibliography (with source-quality scoring)
content:
Help me research the topic below and produce a decision-ready research pack.

Topic / question:
- [Paste your research question + any constraints]

Context:
- Audience: [who will use this]
- Use case: [policy decision / product strategy / academic paper / market entry / etc.]
- Geography + timeframe: [e.g., US, 2018–2026]
- What I already believe (optional): [bullets]
- What would change my mind: [bullets]

Requirements:
- Prioritize primary sources (peer-reviewed studies, official statistics, filings, standards, reputable investigative reporting). Use secondary sources only to point to primary.
- If browsing isn’t available, tell me exactly what to search for (queries + where to look) and still produce the structure, evaluation criteria, and provisional synthesis.
- Be explicit about uncertainty. Don’t fill gaps with assumptions.

Deliverables (in this order):
1) Sharp research framing
   - Restate the question as 2–4 testable sub-questions.
   - Define key terms and the “unit of analysis.”
   - List 5–10 falsifiable hypotheses (what evidence would support/refute each).

2) Research map
   - A table with: Sub-question | What evidence is needed | Best source types | Likely confounders | How to validate.

3) Search strategy
   - 12–20 targeted search queries (not generic), grouped by sub-question.
   - Suggested databases/sites (e.g., Google Scholar, PubMed, SSRN, OECD, World Bank, SEC, standards orgs, national statistics, industry regulators).
   - Inclusion/exclusion criteria (date ranges, study designs, populations, jurisdictions).

4) Source-quality scoring rubric
   - Create a 0–5 scoring rubric with dimensions like: credibility, methodological rigor, recency, relevance, transparency, conflicts of interest.
   - Show how to apply it (1 worked example).

5) Annotated bibliography (minimum 12 sources)
   For each source provide:
   - Full citation + link
   - 2–4 sentence annotation (what it says, why it matters)
   - Methods/inputs used (dataset, sample size, design)
   - Key numbers/estimates (with units)
   - Limitations + potential bias
   - Quality score (using your rubric)

6) Evidence synthesis
   - Combine findings into 5–10 bullet conclusions.
   - For each conclusion: strength of evidence (High/Med/Low) + what would increase confidence.
   - Call out contradictions across sources and plausible reasons (measurement differences, selection bias, causality vs correlation).

7) Decision implications
   - 3–5 “so what” implications tailored to my use case.
   - Risks, second-order effects, and scenarios (best/base/worst) with triggers to watch.

8) What’s missing + next steps
   - The highest-value gaps.
   - A 2-week research execution plan with tasks, estimated time, and expected output artifacts.

Output format:
- Use clear headings.
- Use at least one table in sections 2 and 5.
- Keep writing tight and modern.

Start by asking up to 5 clarifying questions only if needed; otherwise, begin with section 1.

title: Turn a Single Product Feature List Into a Clear Landing Page Copy (Hero + Benefits + CTA)
content:
Write landing page copy for the product described below. Keep it clear, practical, and easy to skim. Avoid hype, jargon, and vague claims. If a claim needs proof, either soften it or turn it into a benefit statement without numbers.

Product details (paste):
- Product name:
- Target customer:
- Customer problem (what they struggle with):
- Key features (bullet list):
- Differentiators (why choose this over alternatives):
- Pricing/plan notes (optional):
- Primary call-to-action (signup, book demo, buy, etc.):
- Any constraints (regulated industry, no superlatives, etc.):

Output exactly in this structure:
1) Hero section
- Headline (max 9 words)
- Subheadline (1–2 sentences)
- Primary CTA button text (2–4 words)
- Secondary CTA text (optional)

2) Benefits section
- 5 benefit bullets. Each bullet must:
  - start with a strong verb
  - be no more than 14 words
  - map to at least one feature

3) “How it works”
- 3 steps, each step: title (3–5 words) + one sentence

4) Objection handling
- 4 common objections from this customer
- For each: a practical response (1–2 sentences)

5) Credibility
- 3 trust builders (choose from: guarantee, security/compliance, support, case study angle, integration note). No fake logos or fabricated stats.

6) Final CTA
- 1 short paragraph (2–3 sentences)
- Repeat CTA button text

Before writing, list:
- The single most important outcome for the customer (one sentence)
- The single clearest differentiator (one sentence)

If any essential input is missing, make the smallest reasonable assumption and label it “Assumption:”.

title: Build a research lesson rubric + quiz generator with mastery thresholds
content:
Act as a learning-ops designer for a research methods course. I will paste a topic, audience level, and timebox. Produce a reusable system: a lesson rubric, a mastery map, and an auto-generated quiz bank with grading logic.

Inputs (I will provide):
- Topic / skill: 
- Audience level (HS / undergrad / grad / professional): 
- Timebox (minutes or sessions): 
- Constraints (tools allowed, citations required, solo/group, open/closed book): 
- Common failure modes I’ve observed (optional): 

Output format (use these exact headings):
1) Learning Objectives (3–6)
- Write measurable objectives using action verbs.
- Tag each as: Concept / Procedure / Judgment.

2) Prerequisite Check (5-minute diagnostic)
- 4 questions that detect missing prerequisites.
- For each question: correct answer + what a wrong answer implies.

3) Lesson Flow Logic (operator-ready)
- A step-by-step flow with time estimates.
- Include decision points (IF diagnostic shows X, THEN do Y).
- Include an “abort/deferral rule” if prerequisites are not met.

4) Rubric (mastery-based)
- 4 performance levels: Not Yet / Developing / Proficient / Advanced.
- 5–8 criteria aligned to objectives.
- For each criterion: observable evidence, common pitfalls, and “what to coach next.”

5) Mastery Thresholds + Retake Policy
- Define what counts as mastery (e.g., % score + must-pass criteria).
- Retake rules: what changes, what stays, and how to prevent memorization.

6) Quiz Bank (auto-generated)
Create 18 questions total, grouped as:
- 6 Recall & Definitions
- 6 Application (scenario-based)
- 4 Critical Evaluation (spot flaws, bias, confounds, validity threats)
- 2 Synthesis (design or critique a mini-study)
For each question provide:
- Question text
- Answer key
- Rationale (1–3 sentences)
- Difficulty (Easy/Medium/Hard)
- Objective tags (from section 1)

7) Grading & Feedback Rules (machine-usable)
- Provide a JSON object with:
  - "scoring": points per question type, partial credit rules
  - "common_errors": list of error codes with triggers and feedback snippets
  - "rubric_mapping": how quiz outcomes map to rubric criteria
  - "mastery_decision": logic for Pass/Needs Review/Retake

8) Integrity & Edge Cases
- How to handle: guessing patterns, very short answers, overly verbose answers, use of external tools, ambiguous responses.

Style constraints:
- Keep it professional and concise.
- Avoid long narrative explanations.
- Ensure every rubric criterion is directly assessable by the quiz or an observable task.

Start by asking me for any missing inputs, then generate the full system.
